for the next 10 seconds (when ended, use idle_forever) ...
use `MicGainManager::process` on that buffer over and over again as fast as possible. count how many time this has been done
after the 10 seconds, report how many times the process has been done, and vs how many times it should have been done if it was a realistic 16 kHz stream for 10 seconds

agent_reply: - Creates a `240` sample mono buffer matching `g_mono_buffer`.
- Fills it once with deterministic random PCM.
- Runs `MicGainManager::process()` as fast as possible for 10 seconds.
- Prints actual process count, expected real-time count for 16 kHz / 240-sample buffers, calls/sec, and real-time factor.

user_prompt: Initialize serial port for printing, and also AudioManager and AudioFileRecorder, that should also mean MicGainManager is initialized, as that is what this test is about
Make sure the ClockAgent is synced to compiler time
Start a recording but not using the real hardware, instead
synthesize a buffer that is roughly a 440 Hz tone, roughly, it starts and ends at 0 phase
it has a peak-to-peak that is only 10% of full scale and a DC offset of -10% of full scale
we need to generate this every loop
every loop, queue an unmodified copy of this into the bt2file FIFO, queue a MicGainManager processed copy into the mic2file FIFO, then let the AudioFileRecorder write it into the file
do this such that 10 seconds worth of audio is submit

I will feed the result into the decoder, if everything works, I will see one channel with an unmodified tone, and one channel with the filtered tone, with the DC offset disappearing, and the amplitude growing up to 3X the original
I can run this test over and over again with different parameters to see different responses
Warn me if I've made a mistake here
implement what I have written here in the IDE
agent_reply: - Generates 10 seconds of synthetic mono tone at ~440 Hz, with 10% full-scale peak-to-peak and `-10%` DC offset.
- Pumps/stops the recorder and prints the file path plus final stats.
I also added the prototype in [all_tests.h](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/all_tests.h:17) and selected it in [all_tests.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/all_tests.cpp:13).

Small warning: at 16 kHz, 440 Hz is clean over exactly 10 seconds, but individual 240-sample chunks do not begin/end at zero phase. The generated phase is continuous across chunks, so the recording should still be clean.

commit_message: progress on bluetooth but cannot initiate connection, can handle incoming though

user_prompt: Guru Meditation Error: Core  0 panic'ed (Cache disabled but cached memory region accessed).
I (1731) esp_core_dump_flash: Save core dump to flash...
E (1736) esp_core_dump_flash: Core dump flash config is corrupted! CRC=0x7bd5c66f instead of 0x0
E (1744) esp_core_dump_elf: Elf write init failed!
E (1749) esp_core_dump_common: Core dump write failed with error=-1
I (1755) esp_core_dump_flash: Core dump has been saved to flash.
got a boot loop here, and the decoder isn't working
agent_reply: I found the boot-loop cause and fixed the likely “decoder isn’t working” part too.

The crash was happening before app startup, inside ESP-IDF flash initialization. The decoded trace pointed at `esp_flash_read_chip_id()` calling `memcpy` while flash cache was disabled. The problem was that `memcpy` had been linked into flash at `0x401f...`, so the CPU faulted with:
`Cache disabled but cached memory region accessed`
I added a small linker override component:
That keeps only the needed libc memory/string routines in IRAM without turning the full PSRAM workaround back on. After a clean rebuild, `memcpy` moved to IRAM:

I also set `CONFIG_SPIRAM_CACHE_LIBMEM_IN_IRAM=y` in `platformio.ini` and `sdkconfig.defaults`, but the linker fragment is the real fix here because that sdkconfig option is gated by the older PSRAM workaround path.
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest -j 4` now builds successfully. I did not upload/flash.
One small note: the build regenerated `src/version.c`, and the new `components/thefly_linker_overrides` files are untracked, so those need to be included when you commit this fix.

user_prompt: I just did a test with bluetooth, the test went fine, I took out the card to read the file, the file is fine after decoding, both channels
but I forgot to put the card back in, the device is still running
I tried to test again, it gave me a "cannot start recording" error and then went back into the establishing connection waiting screen.

microSD card errors should be fatal, but for that particular error, see if you can do some sort of check to see if the card is plain missing or just full or something, make the error better, but make it fatal
agent_reply: I added a microSD health probe in [MicroSdCard.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/MicroSdCard/MicroSdCard.h:13) and [MicroSdCard.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/MicroSdCard/MicroSdCard.cpp:120). It now distinguishes:

Then [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:103) makes recording-start storage failures fatal. On start it now probes the card before opening the recording file, and if file creation still fails it probes again before choosing the fatal message.
So the case you hit should now stop on a fatal error like:
That avoids the current path where Bluetooth start failure becomes a dismissible error and returns to the waiting screen.
I did not hardware-test the “remove card while device stays powered” case here, so the next useful smoke test is exactly the sequence you just performed.

user_prompt: ### Task: major change to recorded encrypted file format
The current implementation uses nearly standard `*.wav` files when unencrypted (lowest security level builds), this is easy to stream and play back.

The current implementation uses a custom format that uses fixed sized packets but each packet is variable length internally, and it’s all encrypted. While this packet can also carry metadata and can be extended to carry event data, it is highly unlikely that the advantages of the flexibility is worth it over the disadvantage of it being difficult to play back in the same way, with easily calculatable audio durations and the ability to seek within a file. The file size is smaller but microSD card performance benchmarks shows that we do not need the smaller file size.
So we are going to throw out the whole custom format idea. We are just going to make encrypted `*.wav` files but call them `*.rec` files.
The document `developer-docs\recording-file-format.md` has been updated and is considered current.
A lot of stuff needs to be removed from `lib\AudioFileRecorder\AudioFileRecorder.cpp` , but the new `*.wav` writing functions only need very small changes for this to work.
There needs to be two static buffers, one for the size of a chunk of plaintext audio, and the second buffer for the encrypted audio, before it gets written to the file.

Remove `AudioFileDecoder.cpp` and the associated header file completely. Don’t bother editing them to make them work. Benchmarks have shown that they are incredibly slow and the user will never use them. But we will offer live playback, which is fast because the bottleneck is actually just microSD card writing.
The sizes of the plaintext chunk and the result encrypted chunks shall be defined in `inc\defs.h` so the code can use them later, include also the encrypted RIFF header size.
Do not yet edit the playback related code, do not edit the python scripts. We’ll do that later
agent_reply: Implemented the encrypted recording format change.
- Added shared encrypted WAV chunk sizing in [inc/defs.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:12):
`WAV_ENCRYPTED_AUDIO_PLAINTEXT_LENGTH = 1024`, encrypted audio chunk length, and encrypted RIFF header length.

- Reworked [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:90) so security level 0 still writes normal `.wav`, while security level 1+ writes `.rec` as encrypted WAV-shaped chunks.
- Encrypted `.rec` files now write the RIFF header as the first encrypted chunk, then fixed-size encrypted PCM chunks.
- Final partial encrypted audio chunks are padded with silence so chunk math stays fixed.
- Removed the old packet format definitions from `defs.h`.
I did not edit playback or Python scripts for this pass. `lib/WavPlayback/WavPlayback.h` is still dirty from the earlier shared `WAV_RIFF_HEADER_LENGTH` change, not from this task.
- Rebuilt `thefly_sec1` again after the final retry-path tweak; passed.
- Search confirms old packet/decoder identifiers are gone from code.
One note: `git diff --check` only complains about trailing whitespace in the already-updated `developer-docs/recording-file-format.md`; I left that doc untouched.
Implemented the encrypted recording format change.
- Added shared encrypted WAV chunk sizing in [inc/defs.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:12):
`WAV_ENCRYPTED_AUDIO_PLAINTEXT_LENGTH = 1024`, encrypted audio chunk length, and encrypted RIFF header length.

- Reworked [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:90) so security level 0 still writes normal `.wav`, while security level 1+ writes `.rec` as encrypted WAV-shaped chunks.
- Encrypted `.rec` files now write the RIFF header as the first encrypted chunk, then fixed-size encrypted PCM chunks.
- Final partial encrypted audio chunks are padded with silence so chunk math stays fixed.
- Removed the old packet format definitions from `defs.h`.
I did not edit playback or Python scripts for this pass. `lib/WavPlayback/WavPlayback.h` is still dirty from the earlier shared `WAV_RIFF_HEADER_LENGTH` change, not from this task.
- Rebuilt `thefly_sec1` again after the final retry-path tweak; passed.
- Search confirms old packet/decoder identifiers are gone from code.
One note: `git diff --check` only complains about trailing whitespace in the already-updated `developer-docs/recording-file-format.md`; I left that doc untouched.

user_prompt: the serial port won't work, so initialize the screen, use the smallest font
write the first line to indicate the test has started
simply run in a loop, constantly checking `ExtCodec::pllLocked()`
every time PLL loses lock, a counter goes up, write a new line with the new count, do not reset the count

agent_reply: It starts with `AudioManager::configure_i2s_shared();`, then `ExtCodec::init();`, initializes the display with font 1 / size 1, writes the start line, then loops on `ExtCodec::pllLocked()`. Each locked-to-unlocked transition increments the persistent counter and writes a new line, wrapping back to the top when it reaches the bottom of the screen.

I also registered it in [all_tests.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/all_tests.h:33) and added the usual commented-out call in [all_tests.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/all_tests.cpp:29). `pio run -e thefly_sec1` passed, and `git diff --check` is clean.

user_prompt: this one uses the newer ExtCodec, and it starts up with the correct sequence, I2S setup before I2C setup
use a lookup table for the sine wave
feed the I2S using the existing pump mechanisms, like the `test_speaker` test

I'm going to try wiring the headphone jack on the SGTL5000 into a cheap USB mic adapter with a 3.5mm mic input, see what the signal looks like. So only make the sine wave like 1/4 total allowed amplitude
agent_reply: It does the SGTL5000-safe startup order:

The sine is a 440 Hz lookup-table oscillator, queued into `bluetoothToSpeakerFifo()` and fed out with `AudioManager::pump_bt2spk()`, matching the existing `test_speaker` mechanism. PCM amplitude is `+/-8191`, about 1/4 full-scale int16. The external codec speaker path disables the NS4168 path.
I also registered it in [all_tests.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/all_tests.h:34) and made it the active test in [all_tests.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/all_tests.cpp:30).
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec1`: build passed. Only the existing FTP deprecated `available()` warning showed up.

commit_message: ext codec playback with ledc pwm as mclk

user_prompt: I have 5 of these PCBs made with the external codec, they simply plug into the bottom of the M5Stack Core2 running our code. These PCBs all fully work, but there's a noise in the microphone that is amplified by a MAX4466 routed to the line-in-right of the SGTL5000. So these 5 different PCBs each have a different small modification, such as an additional capacitor soldered to a different location, and I want to evaluate how effective each modification is at removing the unwanted noise.
I've been trying to do this by simply recording the WAV files but this is extremely slow. So I want a on-device visualization tool.
The function `test_noisespectrumanalyzer` is a core-1 function. It should spawn a core-0 thread. Following our convention, core-1 is for GUI tasks and core-0 is for audio tasks.
During initialization, initialize `ExtCodec` and `AudioManager` and the LCD screen, we do not really need the whole GUI, just the screen. We will want to use a full screen canvas (M5Canvas).
The audio input should be set to use the line-in-right microphone no matter what.

The core-0 thread is simply pumping in audio from I2S (reading I2S while enqueuing FIFO). Core-1 thread is doing analysis (dequeuing from FIFO), one analysis for one full screen update, but also rate capped at 30FPS.

The FFT for the spectrum analysis is basically... use a 512 point FFT, bin spacing 31.25 Hz, the bottom of the screen is 320 pixels, but use 256 pixels of it, from the left edge, for 256 frequency bins. For vertical scaling, there's 240 pixels available, we will use two different scales
First scale: pick a fixed scaling that makes sense assuming 10% of full scale is the maximum a raw sample could be. We might adjust this.
Second scale: basically a full scale that can fit the maximum magnitude expected.

Use vertical lines for the bars (use white for the small scale, use green for the full scale). If the user touches the screen, then the touch coordinate selects a particular frequency bin, if one is selected, draw it with orange instead.
If the user touches off of the graph area, then deselect the frequency. If the user pressed BtnA or BtnC, then select the left or right adjacent frequency bin relative to the currently selected one.

So this there's about 64 pixels blank on the right side of the screen. Use the smallest font, display the RMS power, and if selected, display the frequency selected and the magnitude at that frequency. While the GUI update is rate limited to 30FPS, rate limit the text updates to 5 per second so it doesn't become a blur.
Remember to use M5Canvas, draw the graph before drawing the text, draw the text right-justified and with a black background so it can overflow into the graph if needed.

Everything is done with best-effort performance. I don't care if packets are dropped, as I am not listening to the sound, I just want to visualize. So use the latest 512 samples in the FIFO always, the rest can be discarded.
Using any built-in DSP helpers is optional, if it is easy then go ahead, if it requires importing libraries or editing `platformio.ini` then avoid it.
As this is a self contained test, try not touching other code files unless absolutely necessary.
agent_reply: Implemented `test_noisespectrumanalyzer()` in [src/tests/test_noisespectrumanalyzer.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_noisespectrumanalyzer.cpp:484). It initializes the LCD/canvas, external codec, and AudioManager, forces SGTL5000 line-in-right, spawns a core-0 audio pump, keeps the newest 512 samples, runs a 512-point FFT, and renders 256 bins with 10%/100% scale toggle, touch bin selection, adjacent-bin buttons, RMS/bin readout, and 5 Hz text refresh.

I added a tiny AudioManager override hook at [AudioManager.h](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h:98) so the test can force line-in-right even if the codec detect state would normally choose the earbud mic.
Build passed. I left the pre-existing working-tree changes/untracked files outside my edit scope alone.
